Vii . of the remission of sin ; what knowledge man natural have thereof ; and how much it be the interest of true piety to he assure of the same . also , of the resurrection of the body . we have prove above against the epicureans , that there be such a difference between vice and virtue , that it be not place , or time , or law of magistrate , or custom of nation that render the one commendable , and the other worthy of blame ; but their proper nature and essence , which abide eternal equal and uniform . we also show consequent , that as the recompense of goodness be congruous to virtue , so vice have a due right to punishment . for otherwise , what be the meaning of all those terror of conscience , which we show above to be natural to man ? and to what end be the providence of God himself ? who shall have take the reward of virtue out of one of his hand , and the arm of vengeance against vice out of the other ? wherefore i take it now for grant ; beside that though i have not prove it , yet they with who i treat at present , do without long dispute yield it . so that i shall pass direct to speak of the remission of offence which merit that punishment , and of what great concernment it be in order to be true religious towards God , to know by what mean to obtain it , and get full assurance thereof . now there be only four motive which induce man to piety and virtue . the first be that of which alone the epicureans boast they make any account , namely , the knowledge of the excellence of the deity ; to which must be conjoin ( though it agree not with the principle of Epicurus , who place the supreme good of man in pleasure ) the knowledge of the natural honesty which be in thing , and which be lovely and worthy to be follow for its own sake . the second be the sense of benefit we receive and profit that accrue from both the one and the other . for when we be favour by the deity with some great beneficence , we ought earnest to be incite to honour he in acknowledge the same . the three be the hope of obtain some compensation for be virtuous and exercise piety . and the four be the fear of incur his indignation , by fail in the respective duty of piety and virtue . as for the first of these motive , we have already show , that unless God reveal himself in a especial manner , man live in a profound ignorance of his nature and his excellence ; in which respect it can be of much force to excite man to piety . and the case of the second be the same . for although the goodness of God be in infinite manner extend towards man , in their creation , and the conservation of their be , and in such a admirable variety of thing which he have create for their use ; yet so it be that none of mankind have ever acknowledge the same as be meet , nor indeed can they , as shall more ample appear . concern the hope of recompense ( not in this life sure , unless very scant and after a fashion that do not correspond to the infinite goodness which be in God , but in another ) all that man can have of it by the light of nature be unquestionable draw from the consideration of the benefit which good man receive in this life , from whence to argue , that he will enlarge his bounty in that which be to come . but diverse thing hinder a man from be able of himself to take from thence a certain argument for the future . for beside that those benefit consist only in temporal and frail thing , and that it be hard for a carnal man to conjecture by they of eternal and spiritual benediction , they be blend with so many calamity , with which our life be continual persecute , that a man can scarce clear behold the goodness of God through the instance of his justice , unless he bring a very great attention thereunto ; which be that alone which , to speak clear , have induce man that have have no other guide in his service but reason , to address to he devotion after their own fashion ( as Statius intimate in this sentence , Primus in orbe deos fecit timor ) which we will undertake to examine . by fear in the present discourse , i do not understand a reverence arise from consideration of the majesty of he before who a man present himself , which when he do not find himself guilty of any o•fence commit against he , may be conjoin with a cordial love . for a thing that be excellent , great , and adorable of it self , natural cause fear , and fa tremar altrui di maraviglia , as the poet petrark speak , make the spectator tremble with astonishment . but this be no impediment to a violent love and a calm assurance in the conscience . but i understand that sort of fear which spring from the sense of a man own viciousness , and from the knowledge of a vengeance which he foresee will follow . now the nature of this fear be such , to speak general , that the great it be , the less there be of hope to escape punishment , and likewise less love than ought to be have for he who indignation and choler we dread . for as the old poet say in Cicero , quem odimus , periisse cupimus . and this be peculiar verify when thing stand in such term as they do between God and mankind ; Vii . that accord to right reason and the old testament , the mean of obtain salvation ought to be such as the christian religion hold forth . i affirm in the precedent chapter that the law of God , and the nature of his justice require that either all man perish universal , or that some person in their stead endure the punishment which they have merit . but because this be the foundation of the christian religion , and the most usual stumbling-stone on which the Jew and diverse other people fall foul , it be requisite for we to discover it something more clear in this chapter . certain , if they will confess the truth , the natural terror of their conscience , when they consider the justice of God serious , will make they acknowledge that nature it self direct they in order to the obtain of solid comfort , to seek out a satisfaction of merit proportionate to the majesty of he to who it be due , and to the demerit of their offence . for not only the Jew , to who God reveal his vindictive justice more manifest , but other who never hear speak of the law , have be invade by they . which how can it be , do not punishment accompany sin as the shadow a body ; and that for a man to be quit from the penalty , it must either be suffer by another , or he must be exempt from sin himself ? and the natural instinct which lead they to offer sacrifice in the beginning , and which be approve and authorise by the law of God under the old testament , be a evident argument of it . for whence be it that soon after sin commit , Abel offer the firstling of his stock in sacrifice , and that this example become so powerful to all the posterity of Adam , that there have be no nation by who the death of beast immolate in sacrifice be not practise ; but only that nature it self teach he to acknowledge what he have deserve , and all other have in like manner follow his sentiment ? so that though they can easy judge that the satisfaction be not proportionate to the dignity of he with who they thus transact ; yet be unable otherwise to satisfy , they offer that which they can , and withal refer it to the wisdom of God to supply the rest ? Moreover it be apparent by several nation mention above , and who name and custom be record both in holy and profane history , as the cananaean , tyrian , carthaginian , egyptian , cyprian , arabian , persian , scythian , cretan , the ancient grecian , ancient Romans , Gaul , and other , who sacrifice live man , that the opinion which Caesar attribute to the druyde , that it be not possible for the wrath of the god to be appease but by offer the blood of man to they , be natural imprint in their soul ; otherwise man , be sufficient prone to elevate the opinion of his fault and flatter himself , partly through a immoderate self-love , and partly by reason of the little knowledge he have of the nature of God , will never be so inhumane animate against his own species , and even against his own child ; which some of those nation be wont to make victims of . and for testimony to this , i appeal to man peculiar thought in the administration of humane justice . how do they detest murderer and robber , and those that give themselves up to perpetrate heinous crime ? and when they observe a magistrate suffer such person to go on in impunity , do not they judge that he be either like they , one of their complice and partaker in their prey , or that he connive at their facinorous action through want of power ? certain , when any enormous misdeed be commit , there ought not damage to arrive to the commonwealth either by the fact or the example ; but there be a kind of detestableness in the deed that of it self cry out for vengeance , the impunity of which blot the reputation of he who have the authority and power of punish in his hand , and bring he into a evil suspicion and esteem . and he that shall more attentive consider the emanation of his own mind , will find that nature have not only endue we with the passion of anger to be inservient towards defend we from particular injury which be offer to we , but also have imprint in our mind a hatred against wickedness which do not particular reach we , which cause we not to be satisfy till we have see vengeance inflict upon the same . but assoon as we have behold they expiate by suitable punishment , our mind acquiesce in the justice do with a kind of satisfaction , and our indignation cease . for corrupt quality and horrid vice in the soul , when they come to be discover by action , they be like ulcer and cancer which hideous deform the visage ; we divert our thought from the former with indignation , and our eye from the latter with nauseousness . now the virtue which be but little in we , be in God in a degree transcendent eminent ; he possess ( as we may speak ) the body of they , whereas we have no more than the shadow . as therefore a good magistrate do|his not only detest crime , because they be detrimental to the commonwealth , but also by reason of the natural turpitude which render vice odious , be it not pernicious ; so that he think he do not satisfy the natural equity of thing nor his own conscience unless he punish it ; and the more upright a person the judge be , the more hatred do|his he bear against vice for its own sake : so God do|his not only punish sin , be then iversal judge and magistrate of the world , because it produce prejudice to humane society , and be a enemy to its preservation ; but also by reason of that internal and essential deformity in it which be so repugnant to the divine nature and the natural order of thing ; so that he can possible prevail with himself not to revenge it . and the more perfect his nature be , the great be this natural detestation he have against sin . but to proceed further ; the Jew consent that man be natural corrupt by sin , and that they have in they from their conception a evil seminary of vice , nonlatinalphabet which ( they say ) be like a mountain to the good , and a straw to the wicked ; that be , they which be immerge in it be not sensible of it , no more than a dead man will be of his burden , if he be bury under a mountain . but they in who God have put some sparkle of spiritual life , feel what a great hindrance it be to their spiritual motion , and be discruciate and oppress with it in their conscience . and we have evince above that this corruption be manifest in all , against those which do not acknowledge the holy scripture . now , can the divine justice ( in their opinion ) allow that man shall arrive to eternal felicity , without satisfaction beforehand and repentance of that natural corruption ? no , sure . and they which acknowledge no satisfaction , do yet confess it absolute necessary to the obtain forgiveness of sin , and beatitude by that forgiveness , that the sinner repent of his offence . i ask therefore , whence they have this repentance ? whether God beget it in they , or whether they have it from themselves ? certain , the book of the Jew teach express , that it|be god who circumcise the heart . and the usual prayer of David , that God will teach he to walk in the way of his commandment , sufficient show , that if he , that be already convert , and a man accord to God heart , stand in need of the work of God in he that he may follow his way aright , it follow that with great reason it be necessary for God to work the conversion of sinner , who be grow obdurate in their wickedness . This , though it be not so plain hold forth in the holy book , be so evident , that even reason and experience make it good . for if Aristotle ( who acknowledge not the vitiosity of man nature , but attribute all his depravedness to custom ) teach that when a man have once contract a habit of vice by custom , it be not in his power afterward to quit it , by reason that vice have possess his faculty , who strength and performance be necessary to rescue he from it , and hinder they from execute their natural ability ; much more be it impossible for a man to disimplicate himself from sin , the habit of which be in he , not by custom only , but also by nature . custom ( it|be say ) be another nature ; yet nature be always strong than custom . how will it be then , when inveterate custom and nature be find together in the same vice ? certain , in such case another force then humane be requisite to reform it . Moreover , be not true repentance the love of holiness and righteousness ? and be not that love also the chief part of beatitude , of which other thing be but as dependence ? Surely , he that deny this , must pass into the school of the epicureans , who place the supreme good of man in pleasure , and make no reckon of virtue , but only so far as it be requisite to a cheerful and pleasant life . which be condemn by most part of the pagan , and will be shameful for the Jew and those who acknowledge their book to be divine , not to condemn . it will follow therefore that without a forego satisfaction to his justice , God may give the wicked the principal part of beatitude without repentance . for since it|be he that give they to be penitent , and that the principal part of Felicity lie in repentance , that be , in the love of righteousness and holiness , God shall give they the principal part of beatitude before he see any precede repentance in they . now if there be nothing in the nature of God which hinder he from give the most excellent part of the supreme good to the wicked without any regard to repentance , why shall there be aught in it to hinder he that he shall not even without that give they the other part of it , which consist only in appendance , to wit , immortality and glory ? which yet themselves account a thing absurd and impossible . nor will it be material here to say , that it be not possible that God shall give this second and less principal part of beatitude , unless man have the former before , because happiness be a natural consequent of virtue ; beside , that there be such a correspondence between they , that virtue be incompatible with misery , and happiness with vice ; and that they be thing of so repugnant a essence , that there be no possibility of couple they , but there will appear a remarkable dissonance in the conjunction . and last , that God may draw near to a sinful creature to amend it , inasmuch as he love virtue in which the amendment of it consist for its own sake ; but can have any communion with a wicked creature to render it happy , by reason the happiness of a creature be not so precious with God , that to confer it , he shall do injury to his justice and his wisdom , which abhor vice and wickedness . for in the first place , if happiness accompany virtue by a natural and inseparable dependence , misery do not less necessary attend vice and wickedness . so that if it be not possible for virtue not to be remunerate with a suitable happiness , it be not less impossible for vice not to be punish with due chastisement , there be a equal necessity and dependence between these thing . and true , he that consider the matter attentive , will find that the bond which conjoin beatitude and virtue together , be less indissoluble than that which connect vice and punishment . for the condition of the creature be such , that it ought to be holy and virtuous , though God have not command it ; and that God have so command , it be bind to obey his command , though he have not propose the hope of reward , inasmuch as it owe all to God , both in regard of the infinite eminence of his majesty , and because it hold its be from he ; the promise which God make of remuneration , and the actual retribution which he perform of the same , aught to be impute only to his goodness and gratuitous liberality . we can pretend any other right towards he , from who we hold all , yea our very be . now that which proceed from goodness , seem not to be of so strait a obligation , but that he which do it be at his liberty not to do it , and especial when the transaction be between two person , the dignity and authority of one of which be infinite above the condition of the other . hence it be evident how , regard be have to the sovereign majesty of God , which advance he infinite above all his creature , and exempt he from all obligation towards they , he may have enjoin holiness and virtue to his creature without promise recompense for it . but the relation which be between punishment and sin , as to the consequence and dependence one of another , be a relation of justice , from the exercise of which it be so impossible for the supreme and infinite dignity to be exempt , that the great it be , the more be it oblige thereunto ; for that , as i say , the love of justice , on which the exercise depend , and the hatred of evil be a perfection in God , inasmuch as he be supreme governor and magistrate of the universe ; whence the great his dignity be in this respect , the more be he oblige to the exercise of justice , in which if he shall fail , it can not be but he must suffer diminution of his glory . again , if there be such a correspondence between virtue and beatitude , that they can be dissever , without appearance of deformity and irregularity , so that the wisdom of God , who do all accord to weight , number and measure , can permit the disjunction of two thing so close unite together ; there be not less between sin and punishment . of which the motion of our mind , which be as obscure shadow of the property which be in God , may be testimony to our self . for if we have compassion of a miserable man , who we esteem not deserve his misery , we be also move with indignation against one that be high fortunate , but unworthy the felicity he enjoy . and if we observe a excellent harmony between these two thing conjoin together , virtue and Felicity ; so likewise we esteem these two extreme well adapt where we find they together , vice and misery . so it be incongruous to the same wisdom of God who commit no dissonance in any of his administration , to permit a separation of these most natural relative . to conclude , i ready concede that god love the virtue and holiness of his creature by reason of its own excellence ; for how can he but love his own image ? and also that it be more precious to he then the felicity , consequent to it ; that be , to speak more clear , that it be more well-pleasing to he that his creature be holy and virtuous , then that it be happy . but at the same rate that goodness and piety be affect by he , he detest the creature that despise they . and if God be natural incline to provide for the happiness of man , because he be his creator , he be likewise natural incline to execute vengeance upon his sin , inasmuch as he be his judge . and true i wonder how any can so much cry up the mercy of God in the impunity of sin , to the prejudice of his justice , whereas historian do so high extol the memory of Zaleucus , for a act of justice do , as it may seem very much to the injury of the virtue of clemency and mercy . this man ( say Valerius Maximus ) have constitute very wholesome law in the city of the locrian , one of which condemn adulterer to have their eye pull out , it happen that his own son be find guilty of the prohibit crime , and consequent obnoxious to the severity of the punishment attend it . but the whole city intercede in the favour of the young man that he may be exempt from the rigor of the law , be a long time deny , till at length Zaleucus be overcome by the petition of his citizen , that he may preserve the sight of his son and the authority of his law , cause one of his own eye to be pull out and one of his son ; thus by a admirable temperament of equity show himself both a just legislator and a merciful father . why do not he suffer his law to lie dormant for one day , to save both the eye of his son for the good of the commonwealth ? or if he will not that his law shall be whole infringe , why do he punish himself , when he be no partaker in the fault ? but his reason be , that have establish law , he account they inviolable ; and this act be record among the most memorable example of justice . how then can any esteem the eternal law of God possible to be violate with impunity ? therefore either it be impossible for man to attain to the enjoyment of Felicity without satisfy the justice of God ; or else it be possible for they to attain to it without repentance . which the common sense of man abhor . object . but possible some may object and ask we , whether the same absurdity will not follow from the christian doctrine which teach necessity of a satisfaction precedaneous to beatitude ? for it inform we that man have not make satisfaction in their own person but in the person of another , who be constitute a pledge in their room ; and it|be god himself that give they this pledge . now the bestow one to make satisfaction for sin , be a effect of infinite mercy and inestimable bounty ; and yet it be not prevent by the repentance of man , but on the contrary prevent their repentance ;
